paragraphs.text: PROJECT DIAMOND
Scope
The scope of work proposed by KPMG LLP and its subsidiaries (“KPMG” or “we”) is outlined below and can be divided into the following workstreams:
Financial due diligence
Tax due diligence
Tax structuring
Other tax advisory services (modelling etc.)
IT and cyber due diligence
HR due diligence
Deliverables & timing
Verbal and email updates: ongoing
Excel databook: TBD
Integrated due diligence report(s): TBD
Fees
Service							Estimated fees (CAD)
Financial due diligence
Traffic light assessment of Sell-side QofE		No cost / KPMG investment
Core procedures					$275,000 to $325,000
Operational assessment (optional)			$45,000 to $60,000
SPA and other out of scope requests		Discounted hourly rates
US tax due diligence					$80,000 to $115,0001
US tax structuring and modelling assistance		Discounted hourly rates
Canadian tax due diligence 				$40,000 to $60,000
Canadian tax modelling assistance			$25,000 to $30,0002
Canadian tax structuring 				Discounted hourly rates
Other tax advisory procedures				Discounted hourly rates
IT & Cyber due diligence				$70,000 to $90,000
HR due diligence					$50,000 to $70,000
1 Assumes one material entity
2Assumes two iterations of the model

Our professional fees will be based on the actual time required to complete the agreed upon work at discounted hourly rates identified for the professionals assigned to the engagement. Neither the amount of our fees nor the payment of our fees and expenses will be contingent upon transaction completion.

In accordance with standard industry practice, our professional fees are also subject to a 7% technology and support charge to cover information technology infrastructure costs and administrative support of our client service personnel which are not included in our professional fees. The technology and support charge covers costs such as our client service personnel computer hardware and customized KPMG software, telecommunications equipment, client service professional administrative support, IT programming professional services and other client support services.

All fees and other charges do not include any applicable federal, provincial, or other goods and services or sales taxes or duties whether presently in force or imposed in the future. Any such taxes or duties shall be assumed and paid by Client without deduction from the fees and charges hereunder.
KPMG WORKPLAN
A. Financial Due Diligence
Core procedures

Unless otherwise noted, our work will concentrate on historical financial information of the latest fiscal years ended December 31, 2022 (“FY22”), 2023 (“FY23”) and most recent trailing twelve months (“TTM Mar-24” or latest sell-side roll forward made available).
Overview
Read available information and discuss with Target management in order to develop an understanding of Target, including its:
History, organization structure, proposed transaction perimeter and management reporting relationships;
Inter-group operations and allocation of corporate overheads;
Production lines capacity, utilization, scrap rates;
Range of products and services; degree of customization; pricing methodology (including volume discounts/ rebates); key contractual terms (incl. ability to pass cost increases to customers);
Research activities and new product development initiatives;
Range of suppliers, significant dependencies; key contractual terms; price volatility for key raw materials (resin, etc.);
Marketing and distribution channels;
Range of customer segments, customer concentration and regions served;
Key performance metrics used by Target’s management;
Financial reporting framework and information systems;
Key accounting policies including: revenue recognition, inventory valuation methodologies, costing methodology, sales returns, rebates, provisions, management estimates, etc.
Critical analysis of the sell-side financial due diligence report (“VDD Report”)
Read the VDD Report and any available updates and discuss with EY and comment on significant areas including the following:
Management proposed adjustments to reported EBITDA (i.e. market rent, non-recurring items, customer chargebacks, contracted services , director fees, bad debt expenses etc.);
Completeness of the scope of work, any scope restrictions encountered, and out-of-scope areas;
Transaction perimeter;
Extent of access to management, quality of information available, and depth of analysis performed;
Procedures performed to form views on the appropriateness of Target’s inventory valuation methodologies;
Views on the perceived accuracy of the monthly/annual cut-off process; and
Key financial risks and uncertainties with potential impact to value.
Quality of earnings analysis

Summarize potential adjustments identified regarding the earnings of Target in the form of a quality of earnings analysis, summarizing the risks that may impact revenue and/or earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (“EBITDA”) including:
Normalization adjustments presented in the VDD Report;
The impact of any changes in accounting policies;
The impact of foreign exchange including constant currency analysis;
Related party transactions and the pricing thereof;

Recent changes to the business to understand the run-rate EBITDA impact (e.g. brand development and marketing expenses, business expansion into other product segments, impacts of capex investment etc.);

Any other large, unusual or non-recurring events or transactions that may have distorted results and not identified by management in the financial statements (e.g. non-recurring purchase orders; potential business disruption due to COVID-19); and
Other potential items identified during the due diligence process.
Revenue, value add and contribution margin trends

Obtain a comprehensive view of revenue, value add and contribution margin (pending data availability) by product line and by customer. Obtain the analysis and underlying transactional data (manufacturing orders database), if available, and gain an understanding of the major trends in revenue and margins addressing areas including:
Historical revenues (gross, net, volume), value add, contribution, average selling price and average cost per lbs by:
product line (e.g. by décor, technology, packaging format, resin type, category/ SKU grouping, sales channel, order size, if available); additional services (if applicable);
customer category (i.e. individual, business);
distribution channel (i.e. online, wholesale, etc.); and
price, volume, mix and cross-sell analysis.
Seasonal fluctuations in demand and monthly sales trends; and

Breakdown of cost of sales including: raw materials, labels (if applicable), scrap/waste/expired ingredients, packaging materials, freight-in, labour expense, manufacturing overhead, packing and outbound freight for product sales.
Operating expenses and SG&A
Obtain and read an analysis of Target’s operating expenses and SG&A and inquire about and comment on:
Breakdown and historical trends of operating costs;
Personnel costs;
Occupancy costs;
Advertising, sales and marketing;
Professional fees;
Analysis of fixed and variable costs of the business; and
Unusual and extraordinary items.
Working capital
Summarize and normalize historical working capital in the business, including:
Comment on the historical working capital trends;
Understand key drivers and trade ratio trends (i.e. seasonality, days sales outstanding, days inventory outstanding, accounts payable days, etc.);
Accrued liabilities and prepaid expenses;
Payroll related funding and timing of cash flows; and
Large or unusual non-recurring items that may have impacted normal working capital trends including potential items identified within the quality of earnings analysis noted above.
Inventory
Obtain and read an analysis of Target’s inventory and inquire and comment on:
Accounting policies relating to tracking and controlling of inventory;
Inventory costing methodology and its consistent and appropriate application during the historical period;
Inventory aging profile at each period-end date;
Inventory turnover trends;
Key components of inventory and costs capitalized (i.e. product cost, labour, freight, other);
Slow-moving, excess, and obsolete inventory; and
Spoilage and reserve calculations, if any.
Capital expenditures and fixed assets
Fixed asset continuity schedule showing additions and depreciation;
Significant near-term require capex and deferred capex, if any; and
Historical capital expenditures segregated between growth and maintenance.
Net debt
Summarize and comment on net debt items (on and off balance sheet) presented by management and other potential debt-like items; and
Consider whether elements of working capital have the nature of and may be reclassified as net debt, and if so, summarize the potential impact of these adjustments on working capital and net debt.
Commitments and contingencies
Inquire about significant commitments and contingent liabilities including:
Minimum purchase commitments under long-term supply and/or manufacturing contracts;
Incentive compensation and employee pension and post-retirement benefit obligations;
Lease obligations to related parties and 3rd parties (if applicable);
Pending or threatened litigation relating to infringement of intellectual property, regulatory, etc.; and
Other off-balance sheet transactions.
Budget vs. actual results and outturn to latest forecast

Obtain the historical, latest interim financial information available and forecast for the rest of the year. Compare actual results to budget and forecast results to prior year.  Interview management about the reasons for significant fluctuations between periods and with budget. Discuss and bridge to full-year FY24 forecast with management. Comment on predictability of current year’s performance and level of confidence in achieving the 2024 budget.
Operational Cost and Margin assessment (optional)

The objective is to review upside and risk factors in the forecasted operating expenses of Diamond. We will diligence Management’s proposed forecasted operating leverage to identify the major contributors to margin improvement and associated risks in the forecasted period. Specifically, emphasis will be on margins achieved in 2023A and the operational sustainability of these margins in the projected margins.
Review the overall appropriateness and sustainability of management’s operating expenses and operating margin, especially critical factors leading to FY23 EBITDA margins;
Review margin contribution and operating leverage and determine whether costs are fixed, variable, or mixed;

Support assessment by performing external functional benchmarking of other players in the industry and related industries from publicly available information, KPMG’s previous engagements and from KPMG proprietary Database;
Test the operational reasonability of management’s cost forecast through the following:
Develop an independent bottom-up build of forecasted costs to test robustness, completeness and accuracy;
Develop a detailed headcount forecast in order to assess appropriateness of headcount to support revenue targets and identify gaps; and
Investigate the impact of operating leverage on the ability of the business to operate and sustainably support revenue growth.
Review of operational KPI trending;
If available, review profitability and contribution by product lines on a fully allocated basis, identifying performance considerations.
Review future opex savings overlays, including their sustainability and any potential cost headwinds not considered;
Review the forecast model and propose potential adjustments to how costs are scaled and adjusted based on findings; and
Provide a concise report outlining findings.
B. US Due Diligence

In connection with the Client’s proposed acquisition of (i) B&T (US) Corp., and (ii) Polytainers, Inc. (collectively, “US Target” or “US Target entities”), we will perform US tax due diligence with a focus on the areas listed below. The scope and fee estimate for our US tax due diligence services assumes that US Target entities are each classified as a C corporation for US federal income tax purposes and file a separate standalone federal corporate income tax return. If our assumptions are incorrect, we will revise our procedures and fee estimate.

Unless otherwise noted, we will concentrate on the last three taxable years and the most recent available year-to-date financial and tax information.  Our deliverable will be in the format of a key findings report.

We will not review the compensation and benefits plans of US or Canadian Target entities unless specifically requested by you (including, for example, compliance of any deferred compensation plans and employment contracts with Section 409A, review of employment contracts for change in control provisions governed by Section 280G, or compliance of any other employee benefit plans with other relevant sections of the Code, or compliance with section 7 of the Income Tax Act (Canada)). Should you wish for us to review these items, the time and fee for this work is in addition to our fee estimate and will be billed at the agreed upon rates.
Interview management and outside advisors to obtain background information and inquire about US Target’s:
History and legal structure;
Tax planning profile/strategies implemented and benefits obtained.
Impact of P.L. 115-97 (tax reform legislation), P.L. 116–136 (CARES Act), and P.L. 117-169 (Inflation Reduction Act) on US Target’s tax position;
Significant transactions (including acquisitions, dispositions, joint ventures, and intercompany transactions) and the tax treatment thereof;

If applicable, changes in ownership and potential existing limitations on the use of net operating losses, credits, or similar tax attributes (this will not constitute a Section 382 or net operating loss limitation study computation);
Tax accounting methods utilized;
Payments made to recipients in foreign countries (e.g., interest, royalties);
“Listed Transactions” within the meaning of Treasury Regulations Section 301.6111-2(b)(2);
“Reportable Transactions” within the meaning of Treasury Regulations Section 1.6011-4(b);
Related party transactions, transfer pricing methods, and intercompany agreements;
Tax compliance and tax examination status; and
Financial interests in or signature authority over foreign financial accounts (FBAR reporting).
Obtain and read US Target’s audited financial statements, if available.
Obtain and read US federal, state, and local tax returns, including:
Income/franchise tax returns filed for all open tax years;
Schedule of state tax attributes, including tax losses, tax credits, tax holidays and incentives, and limitations or restrictions imposed on their utilization (if any);
Prior year tax elections; and
If applicable, state tax allocation and apportionment workpapers and nexus studies.

Inquire about corporate (or asset) mergers, acquisitions, divestitures, bankruptcies, and restructurings occurring within the last five years, including dates and types of transactions and tax basis implications of such transactions.
Obtain and read correspondence from US federal and state taxing authorities, including completed and pending tax examinations, and inquire regarding current status for the prior five taxable years.
Inquire about tax rulings, changes in accounting methods, or closing agreements entered during the past five years or any pending ruling requests.

Inquire about the use of independent contractors, if any, and procedures for classifying workers as independent contractors. Read information available and make inquiries about US Target’s Form 1099 filings.

Inquire about tax opinion letters, correspondence, and memoranda or studies prepared by US Target, its outside tax advisor, or its tax counsel regarding tax planning or significant tax exposure items within the last five years. In particular, inquire about seller’s pre-transaction tax planning involving the transfer of certain real properties to related to Target’s U’ business to facilitate seller’s tax planning purposes.
Inquire regarding the federal and state income tax status of US Target and whether US Target has been subject to federal and state tax penalties.
Inquire about tax sharing agreements or other tax arrangements.
Inquire about the non-income tax compliance and examination status, including:
Sales and use tax returns filed and compliance procedures;
Payroll tax returns filed and compliance procedures (not to include compensation and benefits matters, e.g., tax treatment of stock-based compensation, section 83(b) elections, etc.);
Withholding tax compliance for payments remitted to non-US persons;
Real and personal property tax filings and compliance procedures; and
Unclaimed property or escheat compliance.
C. US Tax Structuring and Modelling Assistance (as required)

Where requested, we may also provide tax structuring, modelling, and other tax advisory assistance. Such work is based on the actual time incurred at agreed upon rates. In the context of this proposed transaction, such service may include (but are not limited to):

Review structure of transaction as presented and advise Client for any tax-related calculations in the Client’s financial model, including forecast savings from Client’s planned transaction structure, investment structure (notes vs. equity) and rates charged on notes.
Research related to and preparation of a steps plan with regard to the acquisition structure.
Any other related tax advice or assistance with respect to the tax consequences of the proposed transaction or activities post-acquisition, including consideration of:
Tax implications arising from the acquisition structure, taking into account repatriation of profits and the tax implications from financing the transaction through debt/equity;
Tax deductibility of transaction costs, unamortized deferred financing fees, etc.;
Tax deductibility of interest expense; and
Applicability of any transfer taxes as a result of the proposed transaction.
Read draft of the Purchase Agreement and offer commentary to Client and its legal counsel concerning sections relating to tax matters based on the results of the due diligence assistance we provide.
Read and comment on US tax aspects of the proposed transaction funds flow.
Comment on the accuracy of tax assumptions adopted in the financial / valuation model, including:

Review of corporate income tax expense, taking into account the relevant prevailing corporate tax rates (and potential changes) and key permanent differences between accounting and taxable income that impact the historical effective tax rates;
Consideration of key tax attributes, including available tax losses & other credits, if any;
Review of tax depreciation calculations, the proper class of the assets, and the opening balance of tax depreciable assets, as applicable; and
Review of calculations related to various financings, including potential shareholder debt and third-party debt.
Any other related tax advice or assistance with respect to the tax consequences of the proposed transaction.
D. Canadian Tax Due Diligence

Our Canadian tax due diligence procedures will be performed with respect to Client’s proposed acquisition of Barret & Turner Canada Corp., Polytainers Inc. and the real property currently held by Barco Realty Inc. (collectively, “Canadian Target”).

Unless otherwise specifically noted below, our scope of work for tax due diligence will be restricted to the periods FY20–FY23 and year-to-date (“YTD”) FY24 (hereinafter termed as “period under investigation "). References to accounts and associated data are for these periods.
Due diligence procedures will be undertaken through discussions with management, and where applicable, Canadian Target’s tax advisors, and through reading limited documents as described below.
Background and General Tax Matters
Inquire with Canadian Target management and/or their tax advisors about:
Overview of business operations and legal structure;
Nature and extent of tax planning arrangements, significant transactions (acquisitions, dispositions, reorganizations) and expected pre-closing transactions (if any);
Nature and drivers of significant tax attributes;
Results of significant tax audits (sales tax, payroll tax, other) including jurisdictions subject to audit, quantum of known or proposed audit adjustments and, status of current/pending audits;
Quantum of non-arm’s length transactions including basis of pricing/valuation, type of transaction and documentation available to support the transactions;
Historical tax positions taken including the quantum of reserves for uncertain tax positions included in the financial statements and the basis for those reserves;
Canadian subsidies and other relief measures pursued if any, quantum of subsidies received, and tax advice obtained (if applicable);
Any current or expected limitations with respect to interest deductions made or allocable to Canadian Target;
Tax treatment of capital expenditures and their allocation to different classes of depreciable property for the purposes of capital cost allowance; and
Canadian Target’s tax compliance status, including internal procedures and use of external tax preparers and advisors.
Obtain and read corporate income tax returns filed by Canadian Target for the period under investigation and accompanying legal entity financial statements, as made available to us.
Obtain and read correspondence from internal and external tax advisors with respect to significant tax matters, including acquisitions, restructurings, and tax strategies, as made available to us.
Obtain and read significant correspondence with tax authorities, including but not limited results of tax audits.
Employment taxes
Inquire with Canadian Target management and/or their tax advisors about:
Canadian Target’s payroll tax compliance status, including internal procedures related to payroll tax compliance;
Whether Canadian Target has been subject to payroll tax audits;
Compliance history for Canadian payroll tax; and
The nature and extent of use of individual independent contractors.
Indirect tax
Inquire with Canadian Target management and/or their tax advisors about:
Canadian Target’s indirect tax compliance status, including internal procedures related to indirect tax compliance;
Indirect tax status of Canadian Target’s revenue, e.g., taxable/non-taxable supplies and recovery of input tax credits; and
Restrictions on recovery of input tax credits.
Obtain and read a sample of GST/HST/QST/PST returns and remittances for the most recent available full taxation year and supporting working papers.
Comment on potential land transfer taxes on the acquisition of Canadian real property.
E. Canadian Tax Modelling Assistance (as required)
Review the tax assumptions for the Base Case Financial Model and comment on the reasonableness of the tax assumptions made, including with respect to the following:

High-level consideration of the calculation of taxable income at the corporate level income for tax purposes, taking into account key permanent differences between accounting and taxable income and the relevant prevailing corporate tax rates;

Review the calculation of tax depreciation and the opening balance of tax depreciable assets, as applicable, in comparison to the undepreciated capital cost (“UCC”) of depreciable assets reported by Canadian Target on its most recent available tax return;
Analyze calculations related to various financings, including potential shareholder debt and third-party debt including the application of the Excessive Interest and Financing Expense Limitation; and
Comparison of the quantum of tax attributes (e.g. loss pools,) factored in the model to the tax attributes disclosed in the most recent tax returns of Canadian Target (if available).
Review up to two (2) revisions of the Base Case Financial Model to ensure that our comments have been appropriately implemented.
Our deliverable will be an email summary of the comments on the tax assumptions used in the Base Case Financial Model.
F. Canadian Tax Structuring
Our tax structuring procedures may include one or more of the following:
Review and provide a written statement of potential Canadian tax risk of Seller’s proposed pre-close reorganization steps plan.
Assistance with Canadian tax input to proposed investment structure, and commentary on acquiring, holding and exiting from the investment in a tax effective manner.
Further research and analysis of the tax implications of the structure, including consideration of:
Canadian tax implications arising from the investment structure, taking into account repatriation of profits and the tax implications from financing the transaction through debt/equity;
Canadian tax deductibility of unamortized deferred financing fees; and
Canadian tax deductibility of interest expense.
Prepare a high-level strawman and relevant tax considerations with respect to acquiring, holding and exiting from the investment under the chosen structure.
Prepare a detailed steps plan in PowerPoint format with respect to the investment structure and provide relevant Canadian tax considerations.

Assistance with implementation including discussions with Client and Client’s legal counsel regarding the proposed steps and review from a tax perspective of the legal documents prepared by the legal counsel.
G. Other tax advisory services (as required)
Read initial draft of the purchase agreement and auxiliary transaction documents and offer commentary to client and its legal counsel concerning sections relating to tax matters.
Any other related tax advice or assistance with respect to the tax consequences of the proposed transaction or activities post-investment, including consideration of:
Tax deductibility or recovery of transaction costs, unamortized deferred financing fees, etc.;
Tax treatment of employment compensation including stock compensation;
Preparing a summary of the timing of relevant tax filings; and
Anything else related to the transaction that may arise around closing.
H. IT and Cyber Due Diligence (optional)

We will conduct rapid, focused review of the overall IT and cyber environment and produce a high-level red flags report with comments on critical IT risks and opportunities impacting the food packaging solutions provider, including:
Material impact of the IT strategy to the business, including key ongoing, planning, or required IT and cyber initiatives; and
Adequacy of the current and future planned IT systems, infrastructure, cyber security provisions, and material IT risks to the business; and
Reliability and scalability of the IT and Cyber environment and potential capex requirements in support of organic/acquisitive growth.
Our work will focus on the following scope areas:
IT Strategy, Roadmap, and Projects

Analyze and comment on the strategic IT direction, alignment, and performance with the business, and the governance and architecture in place to help achieve the objectives. Assess the key current and planned initiatives, including the planned SAP ERP implementation, their relevance to the strategic direction, cost/benefit, likelihood of successful delivery, and impact on the business.
IT Organization and Sourcing

Assess the overall effectiveness of the IT and Cyber organization structure and sourcing model, including size, skills, capabilities and sourcing of third-party vendors and service providers, in support of the current business, and any changes to IT support required going forward.
Core Systems and Data
Assess the effectiveness, functionality, reliability, and scalability of the core Technology systems environment, including:

Manufacturing and Enterprise Resource planning systems – Bespoke or commercial software solutions and platforms utilized for supply chain, finance, logistics, health, safety and environment (HSE); and

Bespoke, legacy and packaged IT systems – other business software, including customer relationship management, collaboration tools like Microsoft Teams, automation tools like KaiNexus, IT service management, and HR Information Systems (HRIS); and
Data management, data warehouses and data analytics systems.
IT Infrastructure

Analyze and comment on the scalability, performance, capacity, utilization, availability, and resilience of the infrastructure environment, including data centers, office and end user equipment, networks and data storage environments. Highlight material limitations in IT infrastructure environment, including Disaster Recovery and Data/system backups.
Cyber Security and Controls

Analyze and comment on the adequacy and any material risks of the IT and Cyber security environment, including architecture, processes (training, testing), and compliance provisions used to secure networks (perimeter, internal), infrastructure, applications, and data against external threats and losses. Highlight material limitations in IT environment, including Disaster Recovery and Data backups.
Cyber Scan

Perform a non-invasive, outside-in assessment using the KPMG Cyber Scan technology of key security risks associated with the Target, helping to detect threats in a Company's network. Focus includes probing internet-facing parts of the Target's IT environment and trawling the web and dark web to identify cyber security vulnerabilities and evidence of past and present data breaches.
I. HR Due diligence (optional)
Data room
Read data room documents and inquire about the Target's practices for all HR scope items.
Management discussion
Support planned discussions with representatives at the Target with knowledge of the HR platform for all HR scope items.
Workforce overview

Determine the composition of the Target’s workforce including full-time, part-time, and contract employees and independent contractors.  Review FTE analysis, turnover, key skills, labour shortages, family relationships and open positions.
Leadership and critical talent assessment with remuneration, impact of change in control (CIC) and retention risks.
Understand incremental HR headcount requirements to operate the business on a standalone basis.
Understand global / foreign resources including visa’s and right to work provisions and costs and risks.
Compensation and benefit plans
Inquire about compensation philosophy and structure (including against the market) and most significant employee benefits (significant in cost and/or value).

Inquire about potential quality of earnings adjustments and run rate considerations affecting key employee benefit and compensation programs (e.g., defined benefit-type plans (qualified and non-qualified plans), defined contribution, health & welfare, bonus, equity-based plans, etc.).
Understand financial magnitude of any HR-related liabilities (e.g., vacation, bonus accrual, etc.) intended to transfer to Buyer.
Understand benefit plan designs, legal entity sponsorship and participation by employees in the various plans.
Understand fully loaded cost for dedicated employees and shared service employees considering payroll taxes, benefits, and compensation (base and incentive).
Inquire about retirement and equity plans in which employees participate.
Executive compensation

Read documents relating to employment, transaction/retention, CIC-related agreements, performance bonuses, golden parachutes, deferred compensation, equity-based compensation, and nonqualified arrangements and comment on contractual transaction related obligations, and retention issues that may be triggered due to the transaction to the extent identified.

Comment on historical annual fair value of equity-based programs in aggregate for each of the last 3-5 years – split by management (or those expected to receive equity in Client, as identified by you) and all others, if available, for planning / replacement purposes.
Obtain support for total intrinsic value of outstanding equity-based compensation (including number of shares granted and historically reported values), before potential tax deduction limitations.
Retention & Succession Planning
Inquire about retention plans in place to retain critical individuals and critical vacant roles including mitigating actions, to identify any issues post close.
Inquire about succession plans in plans for leadership team, to understand timings, skills gaps and investments and actions already underway.
Inquire about lifelong learning programs, fast track opportunities and people leader programs.
Employee and labour relations
Workforce data review including existing employment contracts, policies and procedures.
Inquire about US and labour relations landscape. Including a review of any current and historical labor relations issues and outstanding labor disputes.
HR operating model
Comment on the HR and Payroll infrastructure and services in place across the business.
Determine the type of HR and Payroll services performed by the client in house and which services are outsourced.
Understand the Human Resources IT systems in use and how they apply to the HR operating model.
HR compliance considerations
Inquire about information and consultation requirements and comment on potential transaction risks identified.

Provide preliminary comments on the Target's HR policies and practices regarding common issues including overtime management, maintenance of employment contracts, grant of statutory annual leave and use of causal labors.
Inquire about the severance arrangements and policies in place.
Culture

Review engagement surveys and comment on employee satisfaction and inquire about Target’s cultural analysis, areas of alignment and variances with the Buyer to identify integration risks and opportunities.
Inquire about leadership roles and decision making in driving the culture of the Target.
Note that KPMG does not interpret or provide legal advice.
